article.title: The Death of Tacit Knowledge in the Age of Explicit Algorithms
article.author: Mike Turner
article.summary: AI による人間専門性のアルゴリズム化は単なる効率の話ではない。医療・法・工学・金融横断で具現化された経験的知（暗黙知）を侵食している。冒頭、患者の悪化兆候を察知する熟練看護師の事例が白眉。ポランニーの「我々は語りうる以上を知っている」が、現代の最も警戒すべき形で立ち上がる。AI が知識集約産業のエントリーレベル業務を引き受けることで、新人が先輩の傍で暗黙知を習得する伝統的パイプラインが、中堅に育つ前に断絶している。日本の製造業の暗黙知伝承を「ノスタルジーではなくリスクマネジメント」として肯定的に言及。
article.key_quote: There is a test every experienced nurse learns to perform and almost none can explain. When assessing a patient for early signs of clinical deterioration, a seasoned nurse will sometimes simply know. The posture, the color, the quality of breathing, the particular way a patient responds to a simple question: none of these data points alone trips a numerical threshold, none triggers an alert in the electronic health record, and yet the experienced nurse knows something is wrong an hour before the monitors do. Ask her what specifically she noticed and she will struggle to tell you. She knows more than she can tell.
article.key_quote_ja: 熟練看護師には、ほぼ誰も説明できない一つの「テスト」がある。患者の悪化の初期兆候を見るとき、熟練の看護師は時に、ただ「分かる」のだ。姿勢、顔色、呼吸の質、簡単な問いへの反応の仕方——これらのどのデータも単独では閾値を超えず、電子カルテのアラートを発動させない。それでも熟練看護師は、モニターが気づく1時間も前に「何かがおかしい」と知っている。何を見たのかと尋ねれば、彼女は答えに窮するだろう。彼女は、語りうる以上を知っているのだ。
points: AI による効率化が、知識継承の足場を破壊している逆説
「言語化できない知」を組織的にどう守るか
日本の製造業の暗黙知伝承は「ノスタルジーではなくリスクマネジメント」

angles_hints.sun: 「分かる」を失う代償、ナース事例から始める全体像
angles_hints.mon: 本当に暗黙知伝達は不可能か、新しい伝達経路の可能性
angles_hints.tue: 日本製造業の暗黙知伝承、神山さん経営現場への接続
angles_hints.wed: ポランニー、野中郁次郎、Crawford『Shop Class as Soulcraft』
angles_hints.thu: Jacquard loom（1804）、Taylor 科学的管理、Ford、米国脱工業化
angles_hints.fri: 神山さんへの問い：あなたの会社の暗黙知は何ですか

article.title: India is No Longer A Future Superpower. It Is Already Reshaping The Global Economy
article.author: Weekly Voice editorial staff
article.summary: インドを「将来の大国」と語ることはもはや時代錯誤になった。非同盟の伝統が「戦略的自律性」と「マルチアライメント」に進化し、米欧日豪と関係を深めながらエネルギー・防衛・通商で独自の利益を守る。Apple の 2025年 iPhone 製造はインドで55百万台、世界生産の約4分の1へ。China Plus One 戦略の最大の受益者。IMF 2026年4月推計でGDP約4.15兆ドル、英日に次ぐが、為替変動を考慮した実態経済の勢いは衰えていない。インドが他国の追随ではなく自前の論理で動く世界が始まっている。
article.key_quote: The most important point is that India is no longer waiting for its moment. It is already changing where the world builds products, how companies reduce China risk, how energy flows are negotiated and how diplomatic power is balanced. The next phase will not be about whether India can become a major power. It will be about how quickly India can manage the pressures that come with already being one.
article.key_quote_ja: 最も重要なのは、インドはもはや自分の時を待っていないということだ。世界が製品を作る場所、企業が中国リスクを減らす方法、エネルギーの流れが交渉される仕方、外交力が均衡する形——インドはすでにそれらを変えつつある。次の段階は、インドが大国になれるかどうかではない。それは、すでに大国であることが伴う諸圧力を、インドがどれだけ素早く処理できるか、という問いになる。
points: 大国 vs 新興国の二分法の終わり
「マルチアライメント」という外交哲学
日本のインド観の構造的アップデート要請

angles_hints.sun: インドの「戦略的自律性」とは
angles_hints.mon: マルチアライメントの綻び、内部矛盾
angles_hints.tue: 日本企業のインド戦略、こころみグループ視点
angles_hints.wed: アマルティア・セン、ガンディー思想の現代的読み
angles_hints.thu: 1991年経済自由化、非同盟運動の系譜
angles_hints.fri: 神山さんへの問い：インドという他者をどう聞くか

W3.theme: 縮小社会リーダーシップ

article.title: Demographic Decline and Resurgence in the Aging Century - Grid-level Population Tendency Grasped by Artificial Intelligence
article.source: Nature - Humanities and Social Sciences Communications
article.author: Chao Li, Alexander Ryota Keeley, Shunsuke Managi (Kyushu University)
article.summary: 日本の人口動態を 2100年まで 500m グリッド単位で予測した AI 解析研究（深層学習モデル、300万パラメータ、精度99%以上）。日本の人口は 2055年に 1億人を下回り、2070年に 9,050万人まで減少、世紀末には緩やかな回復で安定。空間的には、2100年までに日本の半分以上のグリッドが無人化、東京一極集中が継続。世帯規模は2020年の2.26人から2100年には1.39人まで縮小、社会的孤立リスクが高まる。外国人住民は2100年には人口の6%以上に達し、3000以上のグリッドが移民マジョリティ地区となる。
article.key_quote: The forecasts reveal that Japan's population will fall below 100 million by around 2055, decline to 90.5 million by 2070, and stabilize at the end of the century with a modest rebound. At the spatial level, more than half of Japan's grids will become uninhabited by 2100, while Tokyo remains the dominant growth pole, attracting migrants from other cities. Household sizes decrease nationally from 2.26 in 2020 to 1.39 by 2100, signaling rising risks of social isolation. Meanwhile, the proportion of foreign residents rises above 6% of the national population by 2100, with over 3000 grids becoming immigrant-majority communities.
article.key_quote_ja: 予測によれば、日本の人口は2055年頃に1億人を下回り、2070年には9050万人まで減少し、今世紀末にはわずかな回復をともなって安定する。空間的には、2100年までに日本の半分以上のグリッドが無人化する一方、東京は他都市から移住者を惹きつけ続ける支配的成長極であり続ける。世帯規模は全国で2020年の2.26人から2100年には1.39人へと縮小し、社会的孤立リスクの高まりを示す。一方、外国人住民の比率は2100年には人口の6%を超え、3000以上のグリッドが移民マジョリティのコミュニティとなる。
points: 国土の半分が無人化する未来の経営的含意
東京一極集中の不可避性 vs 政策介入の可能性
縮小経済における「集中による効率化」の倫理

angles_hints.sun: 縮小と集中の二重構造、AI予測モデルが描く未来
angles_hints.mon: AIモデルの前提への懐疑、政策介入余地
angles_hints.tue: 地方経済の現場、福祉サービスの再配置、こころみ視点
angles_hints.wed: 「場所」の哲学、和辻哲郎『風土』、磯崎新の都市論
angles_hints.thu: 戦後の都市化、過疎化の歴史、列島改造論
angles_hints.fri: 神山さんへの問い：縮小する国土でリーダーとは何か

article.title: Tech Addiction, Distraction, and Our Love-less Moment
article.author: Aaron Cline Hanbury
article.summary: Simone Weil の「注意は最も希少で純粋な寛大さの形」を引きながら、現代テック依存が「愛なき時代」を象徴することを論じる。家族との祝日でさえ携帯の不在が苦しい、という著者自身の告白から始まる。Weil の哲学では「注意 = 愛」であり、他者の存在を認め、その必要を一瞬でも自分のそれより重要と認めることが愛の核心。米国経済が職場の中断・気散じで毎年数十億ドルを失い、2022年に従業員1人あたり720時間が散漫で失われた。Gloria Mark の研究（注意中断後の回復に23分）も引用。
article.key_quote: To give someone attention is to recognize that they exist and to assent that their needs, at least for a moment, are more important than my own. Now, like philosophers are wont to do, Weil packs her own stuff into her terms, and readers in 2023 should remember that Weil's definition of attention only partially aligns with the colloquial and scientific ways we tend to use it. Still, her idea of attention and love seems true. A perhaps trite example: If you've ever shared a table with someone who portaled attention everywhere but to you, then you know that table feels nothing like love.
article.key_quote_ja: 誰かに注意を向けるということは、その人が存在していることを認め、少なくとも一瞬は、その人の必要を自分の必要より重要だと認めることである。哲学者の常で、Weilは自分の言葉に独自の意味を込めているから、現代の読者は、Weil の「注意」の定義が私たちが普段使う意味と完全には一致しないことを覚えておくべきだ。それでも、注意と愛をめぐる彼女の考えは真実に響く。月並みな例だが——食卓を共にした相手が、注意を貴方以外のあらゆる場所へ向けていたなら、その食卓は愛のない場所だと、あなたは知っている。
points: 注意 = 倫理的・愛の問題、という再フレーム
「他者に向き合う」能力の構造的衰退
経済効率の話に還元しない注意の擁護

angles_hints.sun: 注意 = 愛、という哲学的フレーム
angles_hints.mon: 宗教的なフレームの妥当性、世俗的議論との緊張
angles_hints.tue: 「人と人」ビジネス（こころみ）への含意
angles_hints.thu: 注意の文化史（読書、瞑想、祈り）
angles_hints.fri: 神山さんへの問い：ディープリスニングと注意経済の関係

article.title: The autistic view of the world is not the neurotypical cliché
article.author: Katherine May
article.summary: 自閉症者の世界は「欠損」でも「無感情」でもない。それは別の Umwelt（環世界）であり、感覚情報の量と質が神経定型とは全く異なる仕方で立ち上がる場所だ。Katherine May は自身の体験から書く——賑やかな部屋の感覚的洪水に圧倒され涙が滲むほどなのに、神経定型の人々はその同じ部屋を音と光と匂いの雲のなかを淡々と歩いていく。反復運動、社会的撤退、ルーティンへの固執——観察可能な「自閉症的振る舞い」の多くは、感覚過多への合理的応答である。問題は自閉症者の知覚ではなく、それを「治療すべき逸脱」として読む神経定型側のクリシェの暴力である。ユクスキュル的に言えば、自閉症者は別の Suchbild（探索像）を持って世界に向き合っているにすぎない。
article.key_quote: To an autistic viewer like me, neurotypical life can seem astonishingly unemotional. I'm so overwhelmed by the sensory onslaught of a busy room that I'm almost tearful, while neurotypical folk appear to wade through clouds of sound, light and odour, entirely oblivious.
article.key_quote_ja: 私のような自閉症の観察者にとって、神経定型の人々の生は驚くほど無感情に見える。私が賑やかな部屋の感覚の洪水に圧倒され、ほとんど涙が滲むほどなのに、神経定型の人々は音と光と匂いの雲のなかを、まるで何も気づいていないかのように淡々と歩いていくのだ。
points: 自閉症は「欠損」ではなく別の Umwelt（環世界）
観察可能な「自閉症的振る舞い」の多くは、感覚過多への合理的応答
問題は知覚ではなく、それを「治療すべき逸脱」として読む神経定型側のクリシェの暴力

angles_hints.sun: 「環世界」とは何か——ユクスキュル 1934 年の概念を、自閉症者の感覚世界という具体から導入。神山さんが 6/01〜6/17 紙面コメントで繰り返してきた「他者の環世界」という言葉の哲学的根源を提示する全体像
angles_hints.mon: 神経定型側のクリシェ批判——「感情の欠損」「社会性の欠如」というフレームの暴力性。Katherine May の核心引用「neurotypical folk appear to wade through clouds」を軸に、何が「正常」かを決める権力の位置を問う
angles_hints.tue: こころみグループ・ディープリスニング経営現場への接続——顧客の Umwelt への参入、組織内の neurodiversity、聞き手の知覚バイアス。「相手のフレームに回収せず、相手の Umwelt に敬意を持つ」（6/06 紙面）の実装としての組織設計
angles_hints.wed: 思想家——ユクスキュル『動物と人間の環世界』、メルロ＝ポンティ「他者は別の身体的志向性」、オリヴァー・サックス『火星の人類学者』、Ed Yong『An Immense World』。Umwelt 概念の系譜と現代的読み
angles_hints.thu: 歴史——自閉症概念の歴史（Kanner 1943 / Asperger 1944）、戦後の「治療すべき逸脱」フレーム、1990 年代の neurodiversity 運動（Judy Singer 1998）、Temple Grandin の証言、当事者運動の系譜
angles_hints.fri: ★C「Why being autistic can be a lot of fun」（Chelsey Flood, Aeon, 2026 春, https://aeon.co/essays/why-being-autistic-can-be-a-lot-of-fun）へ転換。自閉症者の特別な興味・深い集中・感覚的歓喜（autistic joy / flow）。★B の批判から★C の祝祭への弁証法。神山さんへの問い：あなたの組織のどこに、未だ聞かれていない別の Umwelt がありますか

W6.theme: ウクライナ・ロシア戦争の行方

article.title: ロシア・ウクライナ戦争4年が示す「新たな限定戦争」の輪郭
article.author: フォーサイト編集部
article.summary: 戦争 4 年目に入ったロシア・ウクライナ戦争を「新たな限定戦争（Limited War）」という枠組みから捉え直す。冷戦後の「全面戦争か平和か」という二分法はすでに崩れた。新 START 失効により米ロ間の核軍縮体制は消滅、ソ連崩壊後初めて 2 つの主要核保有国が（間接的にせよ）重大な敵対の対立陣営に立つ事態が出現。中国の核武装進展も含めて、従来の二国間核軍備管理は現実的でなくなった。ドローン、AI、エネルギー攻撃、サプライチェーン分断が「限定戦争」を経済戦争として常態化させる輪郭を、この戦争が浮かび上がらせている。経営者にとって、もはや「戦争」と「平和」のあいだに第三の時間構造が立ち上がりつつある——その輪郭を描く論考。
article.key_quote: ソ連崩壊後初めて、2 つの主要核保有国が（たとえ間接的であっても）重大な敵対行為の対立陣営に立つことになった。
article.key_quote_ja: ソ連崩壊後初めて、2 つの主要核保有国が（たとえ間接的であっても）重大な敵対行為の対立陣営に立つことになった。
points: 戦争 4 年は「全面戦争か平和か」の二分法を破壊し、「限定戦争」という新しい時間構造を露わにした
新 START 失効 + 中国の核武装進展で、従来の二国間核軍備管理は現実的でなくなった
ドローン / AI / エネルギー攻撃 / サプライチェーン分断が「限定戦争」を経済戦争として常態化させる

angles_hints.sun: 「限定戦争」とは何か——4 年経過したロシア・ウクライナ戦争が冷戦後の「全面 vs 平和」二分法を破壊した。新たな時間構造の輪郭を全体像として導入。神山さんが既に紙面コメントで触れてきた「世界の構造変化」を捕捉する概念として提示
angles_hints.mon: 「限定」と「全面」の境界の溶解——核兵器使用閾値の曖昧化、ドローンによる本土攻撃、サイバー / 経済戦争の不可視化。Foresight の核心引用「2 つの主要核保有国が対立陣営」を軸に、何が「限定」を成立させているかを批判的に問う
angles_hints.tue: サプライチェーン / サイバー / エネルギー経営への含意——こころみグループ事業ドメインへの接続。「限定戦争」が日常経営のリスク前提になる時、聞き手の経営者は何を備えるか。BCP / 与信 / 人材確保への具体的含意
angles_hints.wed: 思想家——クラウゼヴィッツ『戦争論』、キッシンジャー『核兵器と外交政策』(1957)、ハーマン・カーン『熱核戦争について』(1960)、レイモン・アロン『戦争を考える』。限定戦争論の系譜と、それが現代経済戦争に何を語るか
angles_hints.thu: 歴史——朝鮮戦争（1950-53、最初の限定戦争）、ベトナム戦争（限定戦争の失敗）、湾岸戦争（1991、限定戦争の成功例）、コソボ介入（1999）。「限定」が政治的目標と軍事手段の比例関係をどう形作ってきたかの系譜
angles_hints.fri: 神山さんへの問い：日常の経営判断に「限定戦争」概念は何を示唆するか。「全面戦争でも平和でもない時間」を生きる経営者は、どこに地政学を埋め込み、どこを切り離すか。W5「環世界」の他者性論と接続し、地政学的他者の認識を経営判断にどう翻訳するかを問う

article.title: Hierarchies have a place even in societies built on equality
article.author: Stephen C. Angle, Kwame Anthony Appiah, Julian Baggini, Daniel Bell 他（Berggruen Philosophy and Culture Center 共同執筆、全 15 名）
article.summary: Berggruen Philosophy and Culture Center（Los Angeles）で 2017 年、Stephen C. Angle、Kwame Anthony Appiah、Julian Baggini、Daniel Bell、Michael Puett 等 15 名の哲学者・政治学者が共同執筆した論考。近代西洋は平等を高く掲げ古い身分階層に挑戦してきたが、階層は消えていないし、完全な平等社会も現実的でも魅力的でもない。問うべきは「階層 vs 平等」ではなく「良い階層と悪い階層の区別」だ。本論考は 6 つの角度から良い階層の擁護を試みる：①官僚制的階層は民主主義に資しうる（少数派保護、党派の暴走抑制、審議の質向上）、②道教的な「power over」ではなく「power with」の階層（馬に乗るように引くと緩めるを繰り返す非支配的力）、③儒教的な相互性——師は弟子が自分を超えることを目指す、④時間とともに動的で domain-specific であること（生涯権力保持と分野越境の「hierarchical drift」への警戒）、⑤敬意のシグナルとしての階層（appraisal respect: 卓越に対する特別な尊重、それは学びと成長への開かれを促す）、⑥調和を可能にする最小限の階層（アフリカ伝統社会や儒教文化圏に echo する minimalist justification）。専門家崇拝への批判もある——複雑な問題は専門家ではなく open-mindedness と reasonableness を持つ generalist 集団の熟議が判断すべきで、専門家は外部リソースとして参照される役割が最良。フラット化議論の前提を根底から問い直し、「階層を消す」ではなく「シグナルとして設計し直す」という選択肢を経営者に開く共同宣言。
article.key_quote: The distinction we should be making is not between hierarchical and egalitarian societies, but between good hierarchies and bad ones. Good hierarchies signal the right kinds of deference; bad ones demand the wrong ones.
article.key_quote_ja: 我々が引くべき線は、階層的社会と平等的社会のあいだではない——良い階層と悪い階層のあいだだ。良い階層は正しい敬意のシグナルになり、悪い階層は間違った敬意を強要する。
points: 問うべきは「階層 vs フラット」ではなく「良い階層 vs 悪い階層」の区別（Berggruen Institute 15 名の共同宣言）
良い階層は「敬意のシグナル」として機能し、appraisal respect（卓越に対する特別な尊重）が学びと成長への開かれを可能にする
道教「power with (not over)」+ 儒教「相互性」+ Age-based reciprocity — 階層は支配ではなく empowerment の形になりうる
動的で domain-specific であること — hierarchical drift（権力の分野越境）と ossification（硬直化）を避ける
官僚制的階層が民主主義に資しうる — 少数派保護 / 党派抑制 / 審議の質向上、効率至上主義への警鐘
専門家崇拝の限界 — 複雑な問題は generalist 集団の熟議で判断、専門家は外部リソースとして参照

angles_hints.sun: 「フラット組織」の理想と現実——階層は本当に消せるか。2010 年代の「フラット礼賛」（ホラクラシー、ティール組織）の系譜と、実装現場での頓挫を概観し、Aeon の議論「良い階層 / 悪い階層の二分」を導入として提示する全体像
angles_hints.mon: 「良い階層 / 悪い階層」の区別——Aeon の核心引用を軸に、敬意のシグナルとしての階層という視点を批判的に展開。「権力の真空」「意思決定の遅延」「心理的安全性の喪失」（HBR 2024 Anicich/Lee の 3 つの落とし穴、副軸として参照）を当てる
angles_hints.tue: こころみグループ実装論——シグナルとしての階層設計。「ディープリスニング = 誰の声をいつ聴くか」の問いと階層論を接続。聞き手企業として、組織の階層を「シグナル」に翻訳する実装。Tomy 'M & A' 体験談、現場 1on1 設計への含意
angles_hints.wed: 思想家——アリストテレス『政治学』（自然的階層論）、Pierre Rosanvallon『La Société des Égaux』（平等社会の歴史社会学）、Frederick Laloux『Reinventing Organizations』（ティール組織論）との対比。良い階層論の系譜
angles_hints.thu: 歴史——ギルド制（中世、職人階層の社会的機能）、Max Weber『官僚制』（合理化と階層）、ホラクラシー（Brian Robertson, 2007）、ティール組織（Frederic Laloux, 2014）、Zappos 実験（2013-2018）—階層論の系譜と「フラット化」の歴史的試行
angles_hints.fri: 神山さんへの問い：あなたの組織の階層は「シグナル」として機能しているか、それとも惰性的な命令系統か。W5「環世界」の他者性論（他者を聴く）と接続し、「誰の声をいつ聴くか」という階層的判断を、ディープリスニング経営の中心に据え直す問いとして提示

article.full_text_excerpt:
The modern West has placed a high premium on the value of equality. Equal rights are enshrined in law while old hierarchies of nobility and social class have been challenged, if not completely dismantled. Yet hierarchies have not disappeared. Society is still stratified according to wealth and status in myriad ways. On the other hand, the idea of a purely egalitarian world in which there are no hierarchies at all would appear to be both unrealistic and unattractive. Nobody, on reflection, would want to eliminate all hierarchies—we prefer to be treated by senior surgeons not medical students, get financial advice from professionals not interns.

Yet hierarchy is an unfashionable thing to defend or to praise. British government ministers denounce experts as out of tune with popular feeling; both Donald Trump and Bernie Sanders built platforms on attacking Washington elites; economists are blamed for not predicting the 2008 crash; and even the best-established practice of medical experts, such as childhood vaccinations, are treated with resistance and disbelief. We live in a time when no distinction is drawn between justified and useful hierarchies on the one hand, and self-interested, exploitative elites on the other. To the extent that hierarchies are inevitable, it is important to create good ones and avoid those that are pernicious.

First, bureaucratic hierarchies can serve democracy. Bureaucracy is even less popular these days than hierarchy. Yet bureaucratic hierarchies can instantiate crucial democratic values, such as the rule of law and equal treatment. There are at least three ways in which usually hierarchical constitutional institutions can enhance democracy: by protecting minority rights; by curbing the power of majority or minority factions to pass legislation favouring themselves at the expense of the public good; and by increasing the epistemic resources that are brought to bear on decision-making. Yet in recent decades, these civic hierarchies have been dismantled and often replaced with decentralised, competitive markets, all in the name of efficiency—but this makes sense only if efficiency is considered the overriding priority.

Hierarchy is oppressive when it is reduced to a simple power over others. But there are also forms of hierarchy that involve power with, not over. Daoism characterises this kind of power effectively in the image of riding a horse, when sometimes you have to pull, and sometimes let go. This is not domination but negotiation. In Daoism, power is a matter of energy and competence rather than domination and authority. In this sense, a hierarchy can be empowering, not disabling. Take the examples of good relationships between parents and children, teachers and students, or employers and employees. These work best when the person higher in the hierarchy does not use that position to dominate those lower down but to enable them to grow in their own powers.

A common Confucian ideal is that a master ought to aim for the student to surpass him or her. Confucian hierarchies are marked by reciprocity and mutual concern. Inequalities of status and power can therefore be acceptable to the extent that these inequalities are embedded in relationships of reciprocity and mutual concern, and conducive to the advancement of those lower in the hierarchy.

Hierarchies should be dynamic over time. Hierarchies are often pernicious not because they distinguish between people, but because they perpetuate these distinctions even when they are no longer merited or serve a good purpose. In short, hierarchies become ossified. All legitimate hierarchies must allow for changes over time in order for them not to lead to the unjust accumulation of power. This is built into the age-based hierarchies endorsed by Confucians, since the young will eventually rise to take on the elevated status and authority of the old.

To protect against abuse by those with higher status, hierarchies should also be domain-specific: hierarchies become problematic when they become generalised, so that people who have power, authority or respect in one domain command it in others too. Most obviously, we see this when holders of political power wield disproportionate legal power. Hence, we need to guard against what we might call **hierarchical drift**: the extension of power from a specific, legitimate domain to other, illegitimate ones.

This hierarchical drift occurs not only in politics, but in other complex human arenas. It's tempting to think that the best people to make decisions are experts. But the complexity of most real-world problems means that this would often be a mistake. With complicated issues, general-purpose competences such as open-mindedness and, especially, reasonableness are essential for successful deliberation. Expertise can actually get in the way of these competences. Because there is a trade-off between width and depth of expertise, the greater the expert, the narrower the area of competence. Hence the best role for experts is often not as decision-makers, but as external resources to be consulted by a panel of non-specialist generalists selected for general-purpose competences.

We can acknowledge that individuals differ from one another in embodying excellence of various sorts, and these various forms of human excellence elicit from us a special kind of positive regard that philosophers call **'appraisal respect'**. Appraisal respect is a form of esteem that we have for those who display certain excellences: for example, for their high moral character, or their great skill in argument. Since excellences are intrinsically comparative, people will inevitably be ranked through these appraisals, and so to honour someone is to regard them as (in some particular respects) better than people who embody or advance the value less.

One reason why hierarchy is offensive to the modern, egalitarian mind is that it implies deference to those higher up than them. Hierarchy can be understood as a signal as to when deference is expected. Good hierarchies signal the right kinds of deference, oppressive hierarchies demand the wrong ones. Accepting that others know more or can do more than us communicates and enables an openness to learning and growth. Deference expresses a recognition of one's finite and fallible nature, communicates both to oneself and to others the centrality of relationality to one's identity and wellbeing, contributes to fluid—and even graceful or beautiful—social functioning.

Historical analysis of Qing China, for example, suggests that gerontocratic hierarchies resulted in a high representation of lower-income groups among political elites. This was simply because life expectancy did not differ much by income, which meant that village elders comprised a representative cross-section of society. Political paternalism—coercive interference with autonomy—is generally regarded with great suspicion for very good reason. But there might be a justification for at least some forms of this, as paternalism can, in fact, foster autonomy. Good paternalistic interventions disseminate knowledge of what is best in forms that are accessible to imperfectly rational agents, and habituate individuals' irrational impulses from an early age such that they later collaborate in the implementation of reason's prescriptions.

Although it would seem to be divisive, hierarchy can promote social harmony. Many cultures justifiably place a high value on communal harmony. Excessive hierarchy works against this, creating divisions within societies. But the goal of preserving communal life means that hierarchy might be justifiable if—and only if—it is the least hierarchical amount required, and likely either to rebut serious discord or to foster a much greater communion. This is a minimalist justification that only ever sanctions the least amount of hierarchy necessary. We can find echoes of such endorsements of harmony-enabling hierarchies in many traditional African societies, as well as in Confucian-influenced cultures in the East.

We are a diverse group of scholars and thinkers who take substantively different views on many political and ethical issues. Recently, we engaged in an intensive discussion of these issues under the aegis of the Berggruen Philosophy and Culture Center in Los Angeles, and we found ourselves agreeing on this: much can be said in defence of some kinds of hierarchy.

article.title: How do we transmit culture when it cannot be put into words?
article.author: Helena Miton
article.summary: 人類の知識がこれほど容易に消えうるのに、なぜ多くの文化的実践は文字記録なしに何百年、何千年と生き延びてきたのか。Helena Miton はこの問いから出発し、文化的知識が記録媒体だけでなく、人間の身体と神経系に宿るものとして整理する。Polanyi が「暗黙知」と呼んだもの——「我々は語りうる以上を知っている」——は、模倣でも言語でも十分には伝わらない。実践が「どう感じられるか」を学ぶ必要があり、それは観察したり指示書を読んだりするだけでは届かない。AI が形式知の処理を高速で担う時代、教育の本質的な問いは「言葉にできない知をどう次世代に渡すか」に帰着する。W1「AI と暗黙知」（産業の暗黙知伝承）の系譜を、文化・教育の現場に拡張する論考。
article.key_quote: Tacit knowledge is what we know but cannot say. Since this kind of knowledge typically requires learning how a practice feels, which can't be conveyed through words alone, neither imitation nor language are much help when it comes to learning this kind of knowledge—you can't simply watch someone, or read some instructions.
article.key_quote_ja: 暗黙知とは、知っているが語れないもののこと。この種の知識を学ぶには、実践が「どう感じられるか」を学ぶ必要があり、それは言葉だけでは伝わらない。模倣も言語も、この種の知識を学ぶ際にはあまり助けにならない——誰かを見ているだけでも、指示書を読むだけでも、伝わらないのだ。
points: 文化的知識は記録媒体だけでなく身体と神経系に宿る——「言葉にできない知」が文化伝達の核心
Polanyi の暗黙知論（我々は語りうる以上を知っている）が、AI 時代の教育論の中心に再浮上
なぜ文字記録なしに何百年も生き延びる文化的実践があるのか——その「伝わり方」の構造を問う

angles_hints.sun: 「言葉にできない知」の伝達——AI 時代の教育の核心問題。W1「AI と暗黙知」（産業の暗黙知伝承）で扱った問題を、教育・文化の現場で再考する。W1 系譜の系列継承として全体像を導入
angles_hints.mon: 「形式知偏重」の学校教育批判——カリキュラム / 評価試験 / 言語化重視のシステムが、本来の「身体に宿る学び」を切り捨てている構造を批判的に展開。Aeon の核心引用「indeed neither imitation nor language are much help」を軸に
angles_hints.tue: こころみグループ実装論——ディープリスニング経営 = 「暗黙知の伝達現場」、聞き手企業として、若手社員への伝達設計。OJT / メンタリング / ペアワーク等の現場で「言葉にできない知」を渡す仕組み。W1 note「暗黙知を継ぐのは誰か」（既公開）の延長
angles_hints.wed: 思想家——Michael Polanyi『暗黙知の次元』(1966)、Marcel Mauss『身体技法』(1934)、Pierre Bourdieu『実践感覚』(1980, ハビトゥス論)、Lev Vygotsky『思考と言語』(1934, 最近接発達領域)、野中郁次郎『知識創造企業』(1995)
angles_hints.thu: 歴史——中世徒弟制（職人ギルドの暗黙知伝承）、近代公教育（プロイセン式の制度化、19 世紀）、John Dewey『民主主義と教育』(1916, 経験主義)、Ivan Illich『脱学校論』(1971)、Paulo Freire『被抑圧者の教育学』(1968)、Howard Gardner 多重知能論 (1983)——「暗黙知を渡す制度」の系譜
angles_hints.fri: 神山さんへの問い：あなたの組織で「言葉にできない知」を渡す現場はどこにあるか、AI 時代にどう守るか。W1 で扱った「ベテランの判断」が消えていく問題を、子の世代に渡すという視座から問い直す。W7「階層 = シグナル」論との接続：暗黙知の伝達には「誰がいつ敬意を払うべきか」という階層的判断が不可欠

article.full_text_excerpt:
You can't speak like they spoke. You can't farm like they farmed. You can't dance, or heal wounds, or greet people like they did. In fact, most of the cultural practices your distant ancestors learned have not reached you. They were lost somewhere along the way – failed transmissions that never crossed the generational chains of knowledge that connect us to our past. Those chains, it turns out, can be incredibly brittle. Without physical records, cultural knowledge can easily break down and disappear.

Think of the music of Mesopotamia, from around 2000 BCE. The only reason we can still attempt to play it today is because ancient Sumerians inscribed the notation for their songs into stone tablets. Think of the hunting techniques of our Palaeolithic ancestors. The only reason we have an idea about what these techniques involved is because we unearthed their carved weapons and tools. Museums are filled with these enduring messages about past cultural practices, coded into artefacts and ruins, or written onto parchment, papyrus and other kinds of media.

These messages can leave us lamenting the uncountable broken chains that separate us from the past: what might we know if more had been recorded? Surely, if our ancestors had just given us written instructions on how to speak, farm, cook, dance, and make music, we could have also learned and transmitted that knowledge. And imagine if they had the recording devices we have today. With a smartphone, they might have recorded the mundane details of their lives, describing their skills in a way that could be easily mastered and shared. The problem, however, is that culture doesn't always work that way. Not everything can be put into words. Who hasn't been frustrated when confronted with recipes instructing you to 'cook until done', 'simmer until thickened' or any other equally ambiguous instruction? And who hasn't been frustrated when trying to imitate someone demonstrating a skill that requires some previous experience, some tacit knowledge? Not everything can be understood simply by watching someone else do it. Some cultural practices can be learned only by doing. They must be felt.

This is what makes cultural chains so brittle. It is why instrument makers in Europe can no longer produce violins like Antonio Stradivarius despite having closely studied the instruments he made, why builders can no longer replicate the stone-fitting techniques of the Inca despite having the necessary tools, and why perfume makers can no longer produce ancient perfumes, despite having the recipes. It is also why I, a French cognitive scientist in my early 30s, am unable to do many of the things that my ancestors once did, including illuminating manuscripts with immaculate handwriting, preparing herbal remedies, hunting with a bow, or making flint tools.

Though our collective forgetting is enormous, it is mostly unremarkable to those who study the transmission of culture. What puzzles me, and others who study transmission, is why so much unwritten knowledge has survived. Despite the brittleness of cultural practices, skills proliferate with and without records, chaining generation to generation, and binding us to our ancestors in deep time. So how do these practices persist if the paths of transmission are so brittle? How has anything at all been transmitted without physical records?

Answering these questions will help us understand how much of our current culture could be transmitted to the future. Though we are living in a time in which cultural knowledge is being recorded and stored at a higher rate than ever before, there is no guarantee this information will be effectively transmitted. Optimising cultural transmission, I believe, involves more than new technologies, massive digital repositories and artificial intelligences. It involves learning how knowledge is archived in human bodies.

Though culture can be brittle, it is often imagined in ways that make it appear solid and enduring. It is portrayed as an expansive sea, an iceberg, a solid ratchet. When imagined as a kind of sea, culture appears everywhere, surrounding us. In the 1960s, the media theorist Marshall McLuhan portrayed culture as a vast and all-encompassing medium. In such a 'sea', we can absorb information and practices by osmosis, even unknowingly. In the 1970s, the anthropologist Edward T Hall suggested that culture was more like an iceberg: we can see only a small portion of it, the deeper parts lie hidden. And in the 1990s, the psychologist Michael Tomasello explained the 'cultural ratchet effect' in which human learning accumulates over time, like a metal ratchet that moves forward only as we build on knowledge from the past. Imagined in these disparate ways, culture appears as something solid and enduring that moves forward and expands. What is a spacecraft, Stanley Kubrick speculated in 2001: A Space Odyssey (1968), but the distant outcome of the first tools used by our hominin ancestors?

A less stable view of culture begins to emerge when we consider some of the problems that bedevil archaeologists and anthropologists. Though they can look at rediscovered Mesopotamian bread moulds or ancient Egyptian dancing wands or Chinese oracle bones, they can't bake Mesopotamian bread or dance like ancient Egyptians or consult the Chinese oracle. The knowledge possessed by the people who used these items is gone, most likely forever. And this loss isn't simply because the relevant knowledge wasn't written down. These and other findings represent forms of culture that likely can't be recorded.

Around the world, teams of researchers have been engaging with these forms of culture by attempting to learn the methods that people once used to make craft objects. Their work shows just how difficult the task of recreating cultural practices can be. The Making and Knowing Project at Columbia University in New York City has attempted to recreate the techniques described in an anonymous 16th-century French manuscript, catalogued as 'Ms. Fr. 640'. Between 2014 and 2020, the team tackled techniques described in the manuscript, including mouldmaking and metalworking, colour making, optics and mechanics, ephemeral art, printmaking, inscriptions, and impressions. At the Stone Age Institute, an independent research centre in Indiana, a team is trying to understand stone-knapping techniques used to produce hunting technologies such as arrowheads and spear tips. Though practised for millions of years, stone-knapping remains a remarkably difficult skill to learn, requiring extensive training.

Recognising how difficult it is to transmit cultural practices, UNESCO has been working to preserve 'intangible cultural heritage', which includes many traditions that might become extinct as the last remaining practitioners die. Languages also fall within this category: around 3,000 remain endangered. Some, like Aka-Cari spoken in the Andaman Islands, India, have gone extinct only recently. The last living native speaker, named Licho, died on 4 April 2020. But what about cultural practices that are less connected to reading, writing and speaking? What about forms of knowledge that are more tacit and embodied?

Cultural transmission is a term used by researchers to describe the process through which certain forms of knowledge are passed between people. When this knowledge is exchanged, even through passive observation, a 'transmission event' has occurred and another link is made in the chain. To understand this process in action, think of something you know how to do but would struggle to explain to someone else. Perhaps it is a specific movement in a sport you play, or a craft technique, or a social skill like knowing the right way to greet another person.

Now, try to think about how long this cultural practice has been around. Think about how many transmission events might link its first occurrence to the moment when you first learned how to do it. How many generations have passed since the practice started? How many people had to learn it, and learn it well enough to pass it on to the next person, for the chain to keep extending? In some cases, the chain of knowledge might be incredibly long – so long that thinking about the sequence of transmission events might induce vertigo. This extended sequence can also make the chain appear incredibly delicate. It could have broken at any one of its many transmission events. This is what makes knowledge chains paradoxical for researchers: if they are so brittle, how have so many forms of cultural knowledge survived?

Some solutions to this problem have been elegantly synthesised in How Traditions Live and Die (2015) by Olivier Morin, an expert in cultural evolution. Morin argues that surviving cultural practices were never that brittle to begin with because they have one or both of the following features: redundancy and repetition. Both ensure that if a transmission event doesn't occur (or fails), another transmission is still possible. Redundancy ensures that a person can learn something from multiple people in different contexts. Your aunt, for example, might pass on some of the knitting and tailoring skills your grandmother failed to teach you. Repetition, on the other hand, ensures that even if one transmission fails, other events will help you learn the things you missed. For example, you might not have fully acquired your grandmother's knitting skills on the first try, but you master them as she demonstrates and teaches them to you over and over.

An alternative way of explaining the paradox between brittle transmission chains and the ubiquity of surviving cultural knowledge involves focusing on how knowledge is stored, not just transmitted. Cultural knowledge is held not only in records written on stone tablets, papyrus or other media. It also exists in bodies and nervous systems. At first glance, this may appear to make things more challenging for cultural transmission, since this kind of knowledge typically requires learning how a practice feels, which can't be conveyed through words alone. This is tacit knowledge, or, as the polymath Michael Polanyi describes it, what we know but cannot say.

Neither imitation nor language are much help when it comes to learning this kind of knowledge. For it to be transmitted, you can't simply watch someone, or read some instructions. Language is perfectly suited to convey all kinds of cultural things that are mainly language to start with, such as stories, but many things need to be experienced firsthand. And what about imitation? Though it can be helpful to learn by watching someone else doing something, the same rule applies: ultimately, you need to do it yourself.

Suppose that you're watching a master glassblower in order to learn how to make a hand-blown cup. What should you pay attention to for you to be able to make a cup using the same technique? Is it how hard the master blows, or how they position their feet, or their hands, or the way they move the molten glass, or something you can't even see, or all of the above? The gap between seeing someone do something skilfully and performing it yourself is often enormous. To reduce this gap, you would first need to have enough knowledge to know what part of the action to observe exactly. You would already need to know what to pay attention to. Then you would face another, even harder problem: how should you use what you can see (such as the molten glass's appearance) to infer things you cannot see (such as its temperature, or how hard the glassblower is blowing).

Furthermore, the correct action in each situation depends on the context, and this is an important part of transmission, too. In football, for example, a skilful player's moves will depend on the position and velocity of the ball, of their teammates and of their opponents. You could write 10,000 words about how a goal was scored and still not convey enough information for someone to replicate the kick. So how do we successfully transmit 'what we know but cannot say' through our bodies, especially when they are physically limited in so many ways?

Think about an embodied or tacit form of cultural knowledge you are familiar with, such as knowing how to make the right facial expression to communicate an emotion, knowing how to ride a horse or make a tennis serve, or knowing how to hold your cup correctly during a Japanese tea ceremony. Now try to break down this practice into bits. In the case of riding horses – a cultural practice that has been around since 3500 BCE – relevant bits might include things such as the location of your hands, the angle between your elbow and your wrist, or the position of your back and the distribution of your weight on the horse's back.

Now, consider how these different bits relate to one another. As Simon DeDeo and I showed in our article 'The Cultural Transmission of Tacit Knowledge' (2022), a crucial feature of these relationships is constraint: each separate movement or position is limited by our physical bodies and abilities. Embodied knowledge is strongly constrained. Not all combinations of states for the different bits are possible because there are always physical and anatomical boundaries to what you can and can't do in any given moment. While riding a horse, for example, if your posture is very straight or you are leaning back slightly, your hands can be only in a limited range of positions; for example, your arms will likely not be long enough to rest high on the horse's neck. And if your body position changes, and your hands go up, the angle formed by your elbow will shift. Embodied cultural practices always involve physical constraints.

In other words, you can start to think of embodied cultural knowledge as a network of interacting bits that influence one another. Not all bits are necessarily influencing one another in all cases. The position of your hands on the reins may not be related to how hard you are gripping. This is important because it suggests that embodied forms of cultural knowledge might not be as difficult to transmit as we assume. They don't need to be fully explained because our bodies are constrained.

Imagine each bit in the network like a switch that can be turned on and off. When one turns 'on' (say, your hands are high on the horse's neck), others will also turn 'on' (your back will be angled forward) because they are connected. In other words, you need only to fix a few bits to determine the state of every other bit in the network. So, if a learner focuses only on mastering those particular traits that matter to a practice, everything else may suddenly click into place more easily. This echoes something else we observe in real life: experts sharing their embodied knowledge need only home in on those few key bits that are essential. For a learner, the interactions between the bits, as determined by the network, will then influence the remaining bits, ideally creating a cultural practice that is close to that of their teachers. This means we don't need to know everything to learn new embodied knowledge. We need only some of the bits to have a whole, 'correct' practice.

For teachers, the skill of sharing knowledge involves knowing which bits to focus on. In his description of the pedagogical practices used by capoeira teachers, the neuroanthropologist Greg Downey describes their use of 'reducing degrees of freedom'. These teachers can create exercises that, Downey explains, 'place a student's body into particular starting positions, force them to go only one direction, or otherwise eliminate options for motion'. Such restrictions involve fixing certain bits, at least temporarily, so that other bits will 'click' into place, which allows students to feel what it is like to perform a given movement correctly.

To help reveal the network of bits to new learners, and to generate a transmission event, teachers commonly use metaphors as short-cuts: 'follow through' in tennis; 'move your whole weight' in salsa dancing; 'throw your elbow, not your fist' in boxing. None of these metaphors make literal sense. 'Following through' has no impact on the trajectory of the ball in tennis, since the ball has already left the racquet; 'moving your whole weight' happens naturally in salsa with each movement you make; and your fist, not your elbow, is what hits when boxing. However, these instructions are still helpful because they allow learners to fix some parts of their movements. By telling you to 'throw your elbow' when throwing a hook, a boxing coach is helping you align your wrist and your elbow, ensuring your body rotates properly and that you are generating a powerful punch. Good teaching often requires metaphors or creative exercises that go beyond the practice itself.

Sometimes, teachers may engineer constraints or use metaphors, but artefacts and materials might also exploit the networked relationships between 'bits' to transmit cultural practices. These artefacts are usually designed to fulfil a specific function or enable a specific use. Scissors, for instance, are easy to use if you're right-handed and much more difficult if you try to use them with your left hand. More specialised tools and objects act in the same way. When horse-riding, a dressage saddle, for example, allows for specific positions of the pelvis and legs that are different from those allowed by a jumping saddle – sometimes, changing a tool can shift the network of 'bits', facilitating entirely different movement. Materials, like different kinds of wood, earth or stone, also make different actions possible and can help 'fix' some part of the network. Think of the early stone knives and arrowheads that our distant ancestors made from flint and obsidian. These minerals were chosen because they could be reliably worked into sharp edges and points.

Seeing cultural knowledge as a network of bits that can switch each other on and off means that successful cultural transmission can be achieved even when transmitting only relatively little information. In such cases, transmission exploits how movements are constrained. The unexpected outcome of this is that there can be many ways of doing something, and some learners may even develop unique versions of practices. In the history of sports, this has happened many times, where examples of unusual or unorthodox techniques abound. Take Sadaharu Oh's distinctive 'flamingo' leg kick in baseball, or Donald 'the Don' Bradman's batting technique (and exaggerated follow-through) in cricket. They show that new variants can still be effective, even if they don't become the dominant style.

However, in some cases, unusual techniques become innovations that alter future transmissions. One example, again in the domain of sports, is Dick Fosbury's backwards flop in high jump. After this new technique helped him win gold at the 1968 Olympic Games in Mexico City, the Fosbury Flop became popular among high-jumpers, who until then prefered techniques that allowed them to land on their feet.

Understanding how cultural transmission exploits relationships in a network of 'bits' doesn't only help with the preservation of current knowledge. It can also give us an insight into new cultural practices that might be discovered in the future.

In our age of information, more cultural practices than ever are being recorded. As server farms bulge with data and archives swell with books and artefacts, it may seem obvious that our knowledge will be preserved and passed down. But putting our faith in this mountain of data may be a mistake. It is a misunderstanding of the embodied nature of many cultural practices, a misunderstanding of how our ancestors were able to successfully pass practices from generation to generation, despite the inherent brittleness of long cultural chains.

Much of our cultural knowledge simply can't be put into words or recorded. It can, however, be stored in the constrained movements of our bodies. Optimising the transmission of a cultural practice doesn't always require a larger amount of information. It can be achieved by leveraging how some bits influence others in a network, by learning how some objects and materials exploit those networks, and by understanding how teachers use pedagogical techniques.

It is hard to say what forms of culture will exist in another 1,000 or 10,000 years. But if tacit knowledge is still around, then it will likely have been transmitted from body to body, by exploiting our physical constraints. This is how 'what we know but cannot say' might someday link our age with the cultures of the deep future.

W9.category_note: カテゴリ 7『思索と価値観の変化』初解禁（docs/themes.md：Sprint 8 で長期保存プール整備後に解禁条件達成済）

article.title: The interoceptive turn is maturing as a rich science of selfhood
article.author: Noga Arikha
article.summary: 「内受容感覚（interoception）」が、自己（selfhood）の新しい科学として成熟しつつある。心臓の鼓動、呼吸、内臓の感覚——身体の内側からの信号が、自己と他者の境界を形作る。歴史家・哲学者 Noga Arikha は、メルロ＝ポンティの現象学、仏教の身体観、計算神経科学を統合した「反認知主義的神経哲学」として整理する。AI が「身体を持たない知能」として現れる時代、人間の自己はますます「身体の内側から感じる能力」によって規定される。経営者の「肌感覚」「胸騒ぎ」「言葉になる前の判断」を、内受容感覚という最新科学の言葉で読み直す視座を開く。
article.key_quote: Our ability to form a boundary between self and other is a function of our ability to feel our embodied selves from within.
article.key_quote_ja: 自己と他者のあいだに境界を引く我々の能力は、身体としての自分を内側から感じる能力の働きなのだ。
points: 内受容感覚（心拍 / 呼吸 / 内臓感覚）が selfhood の核心、自己と他者の境界を形作る
メルロ＝ポンティの現象学 + 仏教の身体観 + 計算神経科学を統合した反認知主義的神経哲学
AI 時代に「身体を持たない知能」と対比される人間の自己——「内側から感じる能力」によって規定される

angles_hints.sun: 内受容感覚とは何か——AI 時代の selfhood の新科学。「心臓の鼓動 / 呼吸 / 内臓感覚」など身体の内側からの信号が自己を形作る、という反認知主義的視座を全体像として導入。カテゴリ 7『思索と価値観の変化』初解禁の象徴記事として位置づける
angles_hints.mon: 「脳中心主義」の認知科学批判——「脳 = コンピューター」のメタファーが見落としてきた身体の内側の信号。Aeon の核心引用「Our ability to form a boundary between self and other is a function of our ability to feel our embodied selves from within」を軸に、AI が「身体を持たない知能」として浮上する時代の鏡として展開
angles_hints.tue: 経営者の『肌感覚』『胸騒ぎ』を内受容感覚として読み直す——こころみグループ実装論、聞き手の身体的次元。ディープリスニングは耳ではなく身体全体で聴く実践、相手の身体からの信号を内受容感覚で受け取る回路。「言葉になる前の判断」が科学的に裏付けられる経営現場
angles_hints.wed: 思想家——Antonio Damasio『デカルトの誤り』(1994, ソマティック・マーカー仮説)、Maurice Merleau-Ponty『知覚の現象学』(1945, 身体図式)、Andy Clark『生まれながらのサイボーグ』、Karl Friston (predictive processing / 自由エネルギー原理)、Anil Seth『あなたの脳は予測する』、仏教マインドフルネス（ヴィパッサナー、内観）。身体感覚論の現代的系譜
angles_hints.thu: 歴史——William James + Carl Lange『情動の身体起源説』(1884)、Walter Cannon の批判 (1927)、Stanley Schachter の二要因説 (1962)、Antonio Damasio『デカルトの誤り』(1994)、Hugo Critchley の interoception 研究 (2000s-)、現代 interoception 研究の制度化 (2010s-)——140 年の身体感覚科学史
angles_hints.fri: 神山さんへの問い：経営者の身体は何を知っているか、なぜ言葉になる前に判断できるか。W5「環世界 = 別の身体的志向性」/ W8「言葉にできない知の伝達」と接続し、内受容感覚は両者の身体的基盤として位置づけ直す。AI 時代に「身体を持つ経営者」が残せる判断とは何か

next_month_planning.note: 7月分（W6-W9）は2026-06-27〜28 の月次選定セッションで決定済。8月分（W10-W13）は2026-07-25（最終土曜）の月次選定セッションで決定
